turns.content: I'm getting a panic in my Rust game server when processing the player log data. The error says 'index out of bounds' even though I checked the length before calling it. Here is the stack trace: my_log::process_line -> iterator::next -> index error at line 42.

It sounds like you're using an index-based loop instead of consuming the iterator directly, which causes that panic. In Rust, when working with collections like Vec or arrays in game loops, you should prefer methods on iterators over manual indexing to avoid this issue entirely. You can use take() to limit how many elements are processed before stopping.

I thought I was doing it right by checking bounds manually, but clearly my logic is flawed somewhere. Can you explain why manual checks don't help here and what the correct approach would be? I need to fix this fast.

The problem is that manual index arithmetic doesn't guarantee safety if your iteration logic changes later; iterators handle consumption safely. Instead of checking bounds, you should chain take() with skip() or step_by() to create a windowing effect over the log stream without risking an out-of-bounds panic.
